selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion": "I've seen plenty of fake stuff online over the years, even though I don't spend much time there myself. As someone who's been through some tough times in life, including depression, I can tell you that misinformation is like a poison seed spreading everywhere.\n\nMy dad actually has a master's degree and taught me more about critical thinking than formal schooling ever did when we were younger. He always said education isn't just reading textbooks but learning how to spot lies too. That makes sense because growing up, I learned in school that nothing is black and white - even online stuff needs careful checking.\n\nThe fake news thing has been bothering me since it started taking over the world five years ago now. It's like those people who post nonsense are poisoning our minds with half-truths and complete fakes. And because my personality type shows very low openness, I get uncomfortable when things aren't clear cut or based on evidence.\n\nWhat really bugs me is how these fake posts can play into someone's insecurities. For instance, sometimes you see outrageous claims about climate change denial that have no scientific basis at all - it feels wrong to just let them spread unchallenged.",
    "reasoning_summary": "I analyzed the problem by drawing from personal experiences with misinformation and connecting it to my depression struggles where I've learned about spotting lies. I considered how education, particularly critical thinking skills taught by family members, helps combat fake content effectively without relying on high formal schooling levels which aren't present here.",
    "tone": "Somber but determined",
    "citations": ["Personal Experience", "Family Educational Background"],
    "safety_notes": "",

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've seen plenty of fake stuff online over the years, even though I don't spend much time there myself. As someone who's been through some tough times in life, including depression, I can tell you that misinformation is like a poison seed spreading everywhere.

My dad actually has a master's degree and taught me more about critical thinking than formal schooling ever did when we were younger. He always said education isn't just reading textbooks but learning how to spot lies too. That makes sense because growing up, I learned in school that nothing is black and white - even online stuff needs careful checking.

The fake news thing has been bothering me since it started taking over the world five years ago now. It's like those people who post nonsense are poisoning our minds with half-truths and complete fakes. And because my personality type shows very low openness, I get uncomfortable when things aren't clear cut or based on evidence.

What really bugs me is how these fake posts can play into someone's insecurities. For instance, sometimes you see outrageous claims about climate change denial that have no scientific basis at all - it feels wrong to just let them spread unchallenged.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I analyzed the problem by drawing from personal experiences with misinformation and connecting it to my depression struggles where I've learned about spotting lies. I considered how education, particularly critical thinking skills taught by family members, helps combat fake content effectively without relying on high formal schooling levels which aren't present here.
